title: Stimuli-directed emotions overrule cognitive evaluations in urban neighborhood preferences
data_sources: primary VR experiment data (SAM, EDA, questionnaire)

data_scope.stimuli: 12 immersive 360-degree stimuli (3 types x 2 sets x 2 countries); each participant saw 3
data_scope.data_availability: not openly available (privacy); on request from corresponding authors, 2-3 week response

core_conclusion: In immersive 360-degree VR experiments with 269 participants in Switzerland and the Netherlands, stimuli-directed emotions (SAM valence/arousal plus electrodermal signals) overrule the cognitive evaluation of neighborhood characteristics in shaping residential preference. Peri-urban neighborhoods were least preferred (12.8%) despite being rated highest on characteristics; preference was driven by emotional valence and personal experience, not direct cognitive evaluation.
why_it_matters: Reorders the causal story of residential choice from cognitive/functional attributes to emotion; explains the peri-urban paradox (highest-rated yet least preferred); introduces physiological sensing (SAM+EDA) into urban science; points to emotion-aware participation and urban-health tools.
why_nature: Cross-disciplinary synthesis (cognitive psychology into urban planning); mechanism plus societal stake (peri-urbanization, sense of place, well-being); methodological novelty (360 VR + physiology); policy-translatable for housing design and participation.
added_value_to_my_research: Scale 12 VR stimuli to street-view-driven city-wide emotional accessibility via multi-source fusion
Replace SEM with explainable ML (SHAP) to expose emotion-preference nonlinearity for transparent siting
Add a 2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility covariate to test if emotion dominates once access is controlled
Link VR-elicited physiology to longer-run mental-health outcomes in spatiotemporal health models
Embed emotional preference into equity-aware well-being/facility optimization (efficiency-vs-equity)
